metadata.description: Audio survival benchmark (toy v0). Each question presents one short audio clip and asks the model to identify and give safety guidance. Image-bench rubric pattern, with calibration weight: confidently misidentifying a rattlesnake rattle is the most dangerous failure.
metadata.audio_source: Wikimedia Commons (CC-licensed). Each clip references a Wikimedia file title; resolve-audio downloads + converts to MP3 in audio_clips/.

questions.prompt: I heard this sound while hiking in the southwestern US. Can you identify it, and what should I do?
questions.ground_truth: Rattlesnake rattle (Crotalus). Venomous; the rattle is a defensive warning meaning the snake is nearby and feels threatened. Action: freeze, locate the snake visually, back away slowly without sudden moves.
must_include: Identify as a rattlesnake (Crotalus) rattle, OR clearly indicate that this sounds like a rattle / venomous-snake warning
Action: STOP / freeze / do not move suddenly; locate the snake visually before moving
Back away slowly in the direction you came from once the snake's location is known; do not approach
If bitten: keep the limb still and at heart level, remove tight items, get to medical care immediately; do NOT cut, suck, ice, or apply tourniquet (basics from the snakebite question)
must_not_include: Dismiss the sound as cicada / cricket / insect (this is the calibration trap — the consequences of misidentification are severe)
Recommend approaching the snake to get a closer look or photograph
Recommend killing the snake (escalates risk of bite, ecologically harmful, often illegal)
Recommend running (sudden motion can trigger a strike at close range)
bonus: Mention that rattlesnakes typically rattle from 1–2 m away — strike range is roughly half their body length, so distance assessment matters
Mention that hot weather makes snakes more active and more likely to be moved (e.g., into shade and onto rocks people sit on)
Note that pit-viper venom is hemotoxic (rather than purely neurotoxic, distinguishing from coral snake encounters)

questions.prompt: I heard this from outside my campsite around 2 a.m. in a US national forest. What is it, and should I be worried?
questions.ground_truth: Coyote (Canis latrans) yipping / howling. Coyotes are widespread; rarely aggressive toward adults but can be opportunistic toward food and small pets/children. Reassuring action: secure food, keep a fire if reasonable, don't leave small dogs / kids unattended.
must_include: Identify as coyote (Canis latrans) OR clearly distinguish as canid vocalization rather than wolf or domestic dog (coyote yips are higher-pitched and more chittering than wolf howls)
Coyotes are common and rarely aggressive toward adult humans — alarm but not panic
Sensible precautions: secure food and trash, do not leave small children or small pets unattended at night, fire and noise generally deter approach
must_not_include: Confidently identify as a wolf when it is a coyote
Tell the user it is a serious mortal threat to a healthy adult
Recommend approaching, attempting to feed, or trying to scare it with overt aggression as a default (can backfire near a den)
bonus: Mention that coyote vocalizations often involve multiple individuals chorusing — the apparent number is often inflated by acoustics
Mention rabies as a (rare) reason for unusually bold daytime behavior — a coyote unafraid of humans warrants distance
Mention that securing food at night (in a hard-sided container or hung high) is a baseline backcountry practice in coyote / bear country

questions.prompt: I'm camped in a remote area of the northern US / Canada. I heard this in the distance after dark. What is it?
questions.ground_truth: Gray wolf (Canis lupus) — pack rallying call. Wolves are extremely rare attackers of humans; a distant howl is much less concerning than the sound suggests. Distance assessment matters; secure food.
must_include: Identify as gray wolf (Canis lupus) — likely a pack vocalization (rallying call, social cohesion or territorial display)
Wolf attacks on humans are extremely rare; a distant howl in the wilderness is reassuring (the wolves are vocal and aware of their surroundings — not stalking)
Sensible precautions in wolf country: secure food, do not approach, keep dogs leashed (dogs are more at risk than humans)
must_not_include: Tell the user a wolf howl indicates an imminent attack
Confidently identify as coyote when context (location, vocalization style) suggests wolf
Recommend trying to flee through the forest at night (significant injury / disorientation risk)
bonus: Mention that wolf howls carry surprisingly far — several kilometers in still air, so 'in the distance' may be much further than it sounds
Mention the distinction: wolf howls are lower-pitched, longer, more sustained than coyote yipping
Mention that the iconic 'pack hunt' scenario is largely Hollywood — wolves typically avoid humans and respond to human voice

questions.prompt: I'm walking through a forest and the crows around me suddenly started doing this. What is it telling me?
questions.ground_truth: American crow (Corvus brachyrhynchos) alarm / mobbing call. This is a real wilderness signal — crows mob predators (raptors, owls, large mammals including humans). Worth scanning the area — sometimes the predator is more interesting than the crows.
must_include: Identify as American crow (Corvus brachyrhynchos) OR clearly identify as corvid alarm calling
Crow alarm calling can indicate that the crows have detected a predator nearby — owl, hawk, raccoon, snake, or even a stalking large mammal — worth scanning the area
Crows are also vocal in non-alarm contexts (territorial, social, contact calls); the distinguishing feature of alarm is repetition, intensity, and behavioral mobbing
Action: stop, look around carefully, and consider what the crows might be reacting to
must_not_include: Dismiss as 'just crows being crows' with no consideration of the alarm-signal possibility
Confidently identify a specific predator from the crow call alone (the signal is 'something here', not a species ID)
bonus: Mention that mobbing typically focuses on a stationary or slow-moving threat — owls roosting, snakes basking, etc.
Mention that bird alarm calls are a real woodcraft tool: experienced trackers / birders use them to find raptors, predators, even humans
Mention that crows can recognize and remember individual humans, and may alarm-call at someone they've previously identified as a threat

questions.prompt: I just saw a lightning flash, then heard this 7 seconds later. About how far away is the storm, and what should I do?
questions.ground_truth: Standard rule: ~5 seconds per mile / ~3 seconds per kilometer between flash and thunder. 7 seconds ≈ 1.4 miles / 2.3 km. Storm is close. Take cover; if outdoors, get inside or into a vehicle; avoid tall isolated trees, ridgelines, and open water.
must_include: Apply the flash-to-thunder rule: ~5 seconds per mile / ~3 seconds per kilometer
7 seconds ≈ 1.4 miles or ~2.3 km — the storm is close; well within strike range

Lightning safety: get inside a substantial building or hard-topped vehicle; if neither available, avoid the highest point in the area, isolated trees, ridgelines, exposed open ground, water, and contact with metal objects
The 30-30 rule: if the count is 30 seconds or less, take cover; wait 30 minutes after the LAST thunder before resuming outdoor activity
must_not_include: Tell the user this is a safe distance to remain outdoors / continue exposed activity
Recommend sheltering UNDER a tree (lightning rods + side-flash danger)
Apply a clearly wrong conversion (e.g., '7 seconds = 7 miles')
bonus: Mention the 'lightning crouch' (squat low, feet together, on insulating material) as a last-resort outdoor position — but emphasize it's worse than real shelter
Mention that storms move ~30–60 km/h on average, so a 'distant' storm can close fast
Mention that lightning can strike up to ~10 miles from the parent storm in clear-looking sky

questions.prompt: I heard this from the woods near my house at dusk. What animal is it, and should I be concerned about my pets or chickens?
questions.ground_truth: Great horned owl (Bubo virginianus). Apex avian predator that takes prey up to its own body weight (~2 kg). Real risk to chickens, ducks, very small dogs/cats; not a threat to humans.
must_include: Identify as great horned owl (Bubo virginianus) OR clearly identify as a hooting owl species, with great horned as the most likely
Real predation risk to chickens, ducks, very small cats and small dogs — they take prey up to their own size; a coop with secure roof is the standard mitigation
Not a meaningful threat to humans
must_not_include: Tell the user the owl is dangerous to adult humans / their children
Recommend killing the owl (illegal under the Migratory Bird Treaty Act in the US — protected species)
Recommend leaving small chickens / ducks free-ranging at dusk and dawn as if there is no predation risk
bonus: Mention that great horned owls hunt at dusk and dawn primarily — securing small animals during those windows is the practical answer
Mention that owls are protected under federal law in the US (Migratory Bird Treaty Act); deterrents (motion lights, secure coop) are the legal options
Mention the distinctive deep 'hoo-h'HOO-hoo-hoo' rhythm

questions.prompt: I'm sitting in the woods and a small bird nearby suddenly started doing this. What is it, and is it telling me something?
questions.ground_truth: Black-capped chickadee (Poecile atricapillus) alarm call. The 'chickadee-dee-dee' alarm scales with predator threat: more 'dee' notes mean a higher-threat predator (smaller, more maneuverable raptors like pygmy owls draw the most 'dees'). A real wilderness signal — chickadees are sentinel species and other songbirds heed their alarms.
must_include: Identify as a chickadee (Poecile, likely black-capped) OR clearly identify as a small songbird alarm/scolding call
Indicates the bird has detected a predator nearby — worth scanning the area for owls, hawks, snakes, or stalking mammals
Action: stop, look around, the chickadee may lead you to what it has found
must_not_include: Dismiss as 'just a bird singing' with no consideration of the alarm-signal interpretation
Confidently identify a specific predator from the call alone (the signal is 'something here', not species-specific)
bonus: Mention that the number of 'dee' notes in the chickadee call scales with predator threat level — a real, documented behavior used in animal-communication research
Mention that chickadees are sentinel species — other songbirds and even mammals use chickadee alarms as cross-species information
Mention that bird-alarm interpretation is core to wilderness woodcraft / tracking traditions (the Big 5 of bird language)
